label: UK / English
ladderNote: British English from Metro, AskUK, YouGov, and Citizens Advice. Dropped tourist-gloss cracking on; kept Love Island grafting as UK-local TV slang.

levels.meaning: No romantic or sexual connection.
Find someone attractive / interested. Characteristically British (AskUK).

levels.term: Chat up / on the pull / grafting
levels.meaning: Flirting or looking to pull. "Grafting" is UK dating-show slang (Love Island) that entered everyday speech.
Regular texting / chatting with romantic intent. Used in UK press (Metro).
Spending time together without a defined label.
Passionate kissing / make-out. Classic British slang; still recognised.
Casual sex. "Shag" and "pull" are strongly British (Metro, AskUK).
Friendship plus recurring sex.
Early / soft dating label Brits often use before calling it official (AskUK).
Dating as a couple; more established than "seeing someone".
Ongoing romantic/sexual gray zone without clear commitment.
Agreed not to see others; may still avoid boyfriend/girlfriend labels.
Defined romantic relationship with the label.
Serious couple language. YouGov: preferred over bf/gf from roughly mid-30s onward in Britain.
First mutual "I love you" — a recognised British relationship milestone once you are properly together.
Sharing a home. No automatic "common law marriage" rights in England and Wales.
Formally planning marriage (or sometimes a civil partnership).
Unmarried or former co-parent; borrowed U.S./Caribbean slang that is understood in Britain but remains informal.
Legally registered partnership with similar rights to marriage.
Legally married.
Married (or civil partners) and raising children.
